questions.q: In which year was the Battle of Hydaspes fought?
questions.explanation: The Battle of Hydaspes was fought in 326 BCE during Alexander's campaign in India.

questions.q: Which Indian king fought Alexander of Macedon at the Hydaspes?
questions.explanation: The Indian king Porus (Puru) opposed Alexander at the Battle of Hydaspes.

questions.q: The Hydaspes is the Greek name for which present-day river?
questions.explanation: Hydaspes was the Greek name for the Jhelum river in the Punjab region.

questions.q: Which Macedonian conqueror led the invading army at Hydaspes?
questions.explanation: Alexander the Great of Macedon led the army that fought Porus at the Hydaspes.

questions.q: Which animals used by Porus made the battle especially difficult for Alexander's forces?
questions.explanation: Porus deployed numerous war elephants that posed a serious challenge to the Macedonian cavalry and infantry.

questions.q: What was the outcome of the Battle of Hydaspes?
options: It ended in a truce before battle
Alexander won but at heavy cost
questions.explanation: Alexander prevailed, but the fierce resistance made it one of his costliest victories.

questions.q: What did Alexander do with Porus after defeating him?
options: Sent him to Macedon as a slave
questions.explanation: Impressed by Porus's bravery, Alexander reinstated him as a ruler of his territory.

questions.q: In which broad region of the subcontinent was the Battle of Hydaspes fought?
questions.explanation: The battle took place in the Punjab, on the banks of the Jhelum (Hydaspes) river.

questions.q: The Battle of Hydaspes took place in which century BCE?
questions.explanation: Fought in 326 BCE, the battle belongs to the 4th century BCE.

questions.q: For what quality is Porus chiefly remembered in accounts of the battle?
options: His courage and dignity in defeat
questions.explanation: Porus is remembered for his brave resistance and his dignified bearing even after defeat.

questions.q: When Alexander asked the captured Porus how he wished to be treated, what was his famous reply?
questions.explanation: Tradition records that Porus asked to be treated 'as a king', impressing Alexander into restoring his kingdom.

questions.q: Which neighbouring Indian ruler allied with Alexander against Porus?
questions.explanation: Ambhi, the king of Taxila, allied with Alexander and was a rival of Porus.

questions.q: Heavy seasonal conditions complicated the battle; what was the weather like at the Hydaspes crossing?
options: It was a severe winter snowstorm
It was a dry desert heatwave
It was during the monsoon rains
questions.explanation: The battle was fought during the monsoon, with heavy rain swelling the river and affecting the fighting.

questions.q: How did Alexander manage to get a large part of his army across the swollen Hydaspes?
options: By a surprise crossing upstream at night
By building a permanent stone bridge
By waiting for the dry season
questions.explanation: Alexander made a daring night crossing further upstream to outflank Porus's main position.

questions.q: Alexander's beloved horse is said to have died around the time of this battle. What was its name?
questions.explanation: Alexander's famous horse Bucephalus died around the time of the Hydaspes campaign; he reputedly founded a city in its honour.

questions.q: After Hydaspes, Alexander's troops famously refused to advance further at which river?
questions.explanation: At the Hyphasis (Beas), Alexander's exhausted soldiers mutinied and refused to march eastward, forcing him to turn back.

questions.q: Which arm of Alexander's army was especially effective in neutralising Porus's war elephants and cavalry?
options: His mobile cavalry and mounted archers
questions.explanation: Alexander's superior cavalry and horse-archers harassed and disordered Porus's elephants and chariots.

questions.q: Porus's kingdom lay between the Jhelum and which neighbouring river?
questions.explanation: Porus ruled the land between the Hydaspes (Jhelum) and the Acesines (Chenab) rivers in the Punjab.

questions.q: To commemorate the victory and his horse, Alexander is said to have founded two cities; one was named after Bucephalus. What was the theme of the other's name?
questions.explanation: Alexander founded Bucephala for his horse and Nikaia ('victory') to mark his triumph at the Hydaspes.

questions.q: The Battle of Hydaspes is often counted as which kind of event in Alexander's career?
questions.explanation: Hydaspes is generally regarded as the last great pitched battle Alexander fought before turning homeward.

questions.q: Indian chronicles add little detail, so most surviving accounts of Hydaspes come from which tradition?
questions.explanation: The battle is known chiefly from Greek and Roman authors such as Arrian, Plutarch, and Curtius.

questions.q: Which ancient historian's 'Anabasis of Alexander' is the principal detailed source for the Battle of Hydaspes?
questions.explanation: Arrian's Anabasis of Alexander gives the fullest surviving narrative of the Hydaspes campaign.

questions.q: Whom did Alexander leave to hold the riverbank with part of the army while he crossed upstream to surprise Porus?
questions.explanation: Alexander left Craterus to demonstrate at the main camp while he led the flanking crossing upstream.

questions.q: Porus first sent a detachment, reportedly led by his son, to oppose Alexander's crossing. What was its fate?
options: It was defeated and the son was killed
questions.explanation: The advance force under Porus's son was overwhelmed by Alexander's cavalry, and the son was slain.

questions.q: After the battle, which Macedonian officer later became founder of a Hellenistic dynasty and ceded territory to Chandragupta Maurya?
questions.explanation: Seleucus I Nicator, one of Alexander's successors, later made a treaty with Chandragupta Maurya, ceding eastern provinces.

questions.q: Greek accounts describe Porus as a man of exceptional what, adding to his heroic image?
questions.explanation: Sources such as Arrian stress Porus's unusual height and commanding physical presence.

questions.q: A silver decadrachm, the so-called 'Porus medallion', is thought to commemorate Hydaspes by showing what scene?
options: A horseman attacking a war elephant with riders
questions.explanation: The Porus medallions depict a mounted figure attacking an elephant bearing warriors, widely linked to the Hydaspes battle.

questions.q: Within roughly how many years of the Hydaspes battle did the Mauryan empire under Chandragupta arise to dominate the same regions?
questions.explanation: Within roughly a decade, by about 321 BCE, Chandragupta Maurya had founded an empire that absorbed the northwest.

questions.q: How did the wet ground produced by the monsoon specifically hamper one of Porus's key advantages?
options: The muddy field reduced the mobility and effect of his chariots
questions.explanation: The rain-soaked ground bogged down Porus's chariots, blunting an arm that might otherwise have countered Alexander's cavalry.

questions.q: Following his Indian campaign, by which route did Alexander largely depart the subcontinent?
options: Down the Indus and through the Gedrosian desert
questions.explanation: After turning back, Alexander sailed down the Indus and led part of his army through the harsh Gedrosian (Makran) desert.
